rationale: The citing paper discusses Soman and Cheema (2011) in detail, summarizing its earmarking/partitioning findings and its guilt-based mechanism, and uses it to set up the core theoretical problem. It then develops hypotheses about when earmarking can backfire in emergency spending contexts, explicitly positioning this as moving beyond prior work that focused on increasing savings versus discretionary spending. The cited paper is therefore used for conceptual framing and mechanism grounding, not just listed as background.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Soman and Cheema's earmarking and guilt account by testing the consequences of earmarked savings in a different decision context: paying unavoidable emergency expenses versus borrowing. It qualifies that line of work by showing boundary conditions where preserving earmarked savings can increase costly debt and by proposing an auto-replenishment intervention.
